Title: Kids find confidence during 10th year of Sask. First Nations Spelling Bee
Author: Michelle Berg, Saskatoon StarPhoenix
Summary: Eight-year-old Raquel Greenfield won the primary division at the 10th-anniversary Saskatchewan First Nations Spelling Bee, earning a spot at the national championships in Toronto. Seven-year-old Osa Ahenakew finished second, impressing with her confidence and sportsmanship. The event featured about 200 participants from 20 communities, promoting First Nations youth to compete nationally.
Content:
Osa Ahenakew, 7, reacts on stage during the primary finals of the Saskatchewan First Nations Spelling Bee at Tommy Douglas Collegiate. She finished in second place in her age group during the spelling bee's 10th-year anniversary event on March 20.

Eight-year-old Raquel Greenfield’s spelling success came down to persistence. “She would practice every night,” her mother, Megan Greenfield, said. “She would repeat the word to herself until she spelled it correctly… she’s been really intentionally focused.” That dedication paid off as the Grade 3 student from Brilliant Star Montessori School took first place in the primary division at the Saskatchewan First Nations Spelling Bee, held Friday at Tommy Douglas Collegiate in Saskatoon.

The event marked the spelling bee’s 10th anniversary. It began in 2015 as an initiative to give First Nations youth — many from small communities — a platform to compete at a national level. Around 200 participants from 20 communities were divided into three age categories: primary (ages six to eight), junior (nine to 11), and intermediate (12 to 14). Winners from each division advance to the Spelling Bee of Canada’s National Championships in Toronto on May 23, where they will represent Saskatchewan on a national stage. It will be Greenfield’s first time on a plane. Her mom said the achievement is especially meaningful because her daughter is shy and struggles with anxiety in social situations. “I’m excited for her to have that opportunity,” she said. Greenfield said she felt nervous on stage but happy when she was named the winner. She spelled her final word — “domestic” — with a gentle confidence.

Raquel Greenfield, 8, spells a word during the Saskatchewan First Nations Spelling Bee at Tommy Douglas Collegiate on March 20. She finished in first place. 

The runner-up was seven-year-old Osa Ahenakew, a Grade 2 student from Chief Ahtahkakoop School who described the experience as “pretty cool.” She brought energy to the stage, something she credits in part to dance — and the other competitors. “I’m a ballet dancer,” she said, adding she also competes in traditional powwow dance. When asked where her confidence comes from, she points to her peers: “All the spellers.” Her mother, Colby Calliou, said Ahenakew also surprised her by encouraging other competitors during the event. “There was one little kid there that was struggling and she was like, ‘You got this! It’s okay!’” Calliou said they prepared by studying 15 words a day. If Ahenakew got a word wrong, she’d write it down 10 times.

For both families, the spelling bee was about more than results. “I just told her she could do it,” Megan Greenfield said. “It was about the experience.” When asked which word was her favorite to spell, Ahenakew didn’t hesitate. “Milkshake.”

Title: Season ends for hockey Huskies with OT heartbreak at U Sports nationals
Author: Aidan Jaager
Summary: University of Saskatchewan Huskies, ranked No. 1, lost 4-3 in overtime to No. 8 Saint Mary’s in the U Sports national quarterfinals. Despite a late comeback from 3-0 down, the Huskies' season ended. Connor Roulette scored twice for Saskatchewan, but Ethan Ritchie’s goal secured Saint Mary’s victory. Saint Mary’s advances to face Windsor in the semifinals.
Content:
University of Saskatchewan Huskies head coach Brandon Cote credited No. 8 ranked Saint Mary's Huskies for playing "a real hard game" following a 4-3 overtime loss in the U Sports national championship quarterfinals.

One game. One shot. Season over for the No. 1 University of Saskatchewan men’s hockey team. The top and back-to-back Canada West champion Huskies saw their pursuit of a U Sports national title end abruptly on Thursday with a 4-3 overtime loss to the No. 8 and host squad Saint Mary’s in the University Cup national championship quarterfinals.

“Obviously tough right now for our group,” head coach Cote said. “We had, I guess, higher expectations coming in here, but you know, we’ve had a great year and I’m proud of our guys.”

After being down 3-0 late in the second period against Saint Mary’s, the team battled back to tie the game late—only to fall short in overtime in Halifax.

Cote praised Saint Mary’s for their effort, noting, “They played a real hard game,” and acknowledged the unpredictability of overtime. “We had our chances, we had our opportunities, a couple breakaways, couple point-blankers. Their goaltender played well. Our goaltender played well. They had some good chances, and that’s just the way it goes sometimes,” he said.

U Sports rookie of the year forward Connor Roulette opened the scoring for the Huskies with four minutes left in the second period, netting his sixth goal of the playoffs. He added another goal 15 seconds into the third to reduce the deficit to 3-2.

Despite power play opportunities for both teams late in the game, neither could score until the final minutes when Saint Mary’s took a tripping penalty. Just over 40 seconds into the penalty, Chantz Petruic found Liam Keeler alone in front of the net to tie the game.

The comeback was short-lived, however. Saint Mary’s defenseman Ethan Ritchie redirected a shot over Huskies goaltender Nolan Maier to secure the win with four minutes left in overtime.

Roulette, named the Huskies’ player of the game, reflected on the tournament. “Coming into a tournament like this, they give you the No. 1 seed, but that means nothing when you’re in a tournament with the eight best teams left in Canada,” he said. “I just can’t thank the coaches and our vets enough. Just a really fun year, and it just sucks that it got cut short.”

Saint Mary’s will now face No. 4 Windsor Lancers in the U Sports national championship semifinals on Saturday.

Title: Sask. NDP criticizes new Shellbrook school in education budget
Author: Aidan Jaager
Summary: The Saskatchewan government plans to build a new school in Shellbrook, Premier Scott Moe's hometown, amid criticism from the NDP, which claims many other schools are in worse condition. The NDP highlights 13 schools as critical and 132 as poor, arguing the project may be favoritism. Education Minister Hindley states the Shellbrook project has been a long-term priority for the local school division.
Content:
NDP education critic Matt Love said 13 schools are in "critical" condition and 132 are in "poor" condition. The Opposition insists there are hundreds more schools in greater need of funding than Shellbrook, which is where Premier Scott Moe grew up and the constituency he represents.

In the face of criticism over the 2026-27 provincial budget, the Saskatchewan Party government insists there is no favouritism behind the decision to fund a new school in the home community of Premier Scott Moe. Included among major capital projects for the Ministry of Education was the construction of a new pre-kindergarten to Grade 12 school in Shellbrook, located approximately 45 kilometres west of Prince Albert.

According to the budget, tabled on Wednesday by Finance Minister Jim Reiter, the plan is to replace and consolidate the current elementary and high schools into one facility. The Opposition NDP insists there are hundreds more schools in greater need of funding, and questioned whether it was moved near the top of the list because Moe represents the constituency of Rosthern-Shellbrook.

“The premier gets a shiny new school in Shellbrook while students learning in more than 200 others get leaky or entirely collapsed roofs, a lack of space for student desks and lockers, and entire wings that can no longer accommodate student learning,” NDP education critic Matt Love said on Friday. Love spoke to reporters outside Saskatoon’s Ecole Canadienne Française-Pavillion Monique Rosseau, where in January the library ceiling collapsed.

According to the NDP, analysis of government data indicates 13 schools in Saskatchewan are deemed to be in critical condition, 132 schools are deemed in poor condition, and more than 200 schools are in worse shape than the current schools in Shellbrook. NDP Leader Carla Beck said there are 103 communities in the province with a school in worse shape than those in Shellbrook.

“Tell me again this isn’t about the premier playing favourites with his friends and neighbours,” she said. “If there really is nothing to hide, I call on Scott Moe to publicly release all government documents behind the school capital decisions in this bad news budget.”

Minister of Education Everett Hindley insists there was nothing unfair behind the decision, saying a school in Shellbrook “has been a priority for that school division for a number of years.” He noted the replacement is needed due to the age of the existing schools in the community and that it could be several years until the school in Shellbrook is built.

“There’s 27 school divisions each and every year that identify what would be their top three major capital projects within the schools, within their school division and boundaries,” Hindley said.

Title: Canadian Armed Forces members among troops NATO pulled out of Iraq
Summary: NATO withdrew its trainers from Iraq amid Iran-related retaliatory attacks during the U.S.-Israeli conflict. Canadian Armed Forces personnel, part of the NATO mission, were relocated to safety, with Canada reaffirming its commitment to international stability. NATO continues its mission from Europe, and the Canadian government faced criticism for lack of transparency regarding a missile strike on a Kuwait airbase.
Content:
Canadian Armed Forces members and civilians are among the personnel NATO has pulled out of Iraq as the country faces retaliatory attacks from Iran along with other Gulf countries during the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

Canada's defence minister confirmed that Canadian Armed Forces personnel with the NATO mission in Iraq have been relocated and are safe and accounted for. Defence Minister David McGinty stated that the Canadian Armed Forces and civilians serving on the NATO advisory mission in Iraq are safe and in a secure location. He called on all states to respect international law and uphold the principles of international security.

The military alliance confirmed on Friday that it withdrew the last of its military trainers from Iraq and moved them to Europe. The non-combat NATO mission, launched in 2018, aims to train and advise Iraqi forces to help stabilize the country and fight terrorism. The mission involves several hundred personnel from NATO allies, Austria, and Australia.

U.S. General Alexus Grynkewich, Supreme Allied Commander Europe, expressed gratitude to Iraq and all allies who assisted in the safe relocation of NATO personnel. He stated that the NATO mission will continue from Joint Force Command Naples.

Canada remains committed to supporting international peace and stability in coordination with NATO allies. The Canadian government was criticized earlier this month for not informing the public about a reported Iranian missile strike on an airbase in Kuwait where Canadian Armed Forces members are stationed. No Canadian military members were injured. Conservative defence critic James Bezan called the lack of transparency "really shameful," while the Department of National Defence cited operational security reasons for not discussing potential damage or impacts to military facilities.

Title: As Middle Eastern countries struggle to down Iranian drones, Ukraine sends 200 specialists to help
Summary: Following Iranian Shahed drone attacks in Kuwait, Ukraine has deployed over 200 drone specialists to five Middle Eastern countries to protect infrastructure. Ukraine, experienced in countering Iranian drones used by Russia, is also working on selling interceptor drones to Gulf states amid increased demand and ongoing conflicts involving Iran, the U.S., and Israel.
Content:
After Iranian Shahed drones hit an oil refinery in Kuwait, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy confirmed that Kyiv has sent more than 200 military specialists to the Middle East to help defend critical infrastructure from the same type of threat Ukraine has faced for years.

Zelenskyy said 228 Ukrainian drone specialists have been deployed to five countries — Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Jordan.

After the U.S. and Israel started launching airstrikes against Iran on February 28, Gulf Arab states have been targeted with more than 2,000 drones and missiles. Those countries, which have suffered attacks on energy infrastructure, hotels, and diplomatic missions, are also keen to purchase thousands of Ukraine’s interceptor drones, and Kyiv says that "serious agreements and deals" are being worked out.

"For the last week, we had four different requests for interceptors from the Middle East," said Andrii Sych, co-founder of the Kyiv-based UV military cluster, a manufacturer that also collaborates with other drone companies. "So I can say the demand is quite high."

One inquiry came from the United Arab Emirates, but the manufacturer has not officially sold any interceptors yet because it needs an export license from Kyiv and permission to sell defense technology abroad.

Ukraine has extensive experience in downing Iranian drones, which were first used by Russia in the fall of 2022. Since then, manufacturers across the country have been at the forefront of developing drone interceptors—an innovation driven by daily deadly attacks and extensively tested in battle.

Ukraine's homegrown drones have become increasingly lethal, serving as critical tools in the ongoing conflict with Russia.

Title: Palestinian man says Israeli settlers sexually assaulted him in front of his family
Author: Unknown
Summary: In March, Israeli settlers reportedly assaulted Palestinian man Suhaib Abu Kbash in Humsah, tying his genitals and parading him naked in front of his family. Seven suspects were arrested, and the incident is under investigation. Violence by settlers in the West Bank has increased, with rights groups condemning the Israeli government’s inaction amid ongoing tensions.
Content:
Israeli settlers sexually assaulted a Palestinian man—tying his genitalia with zip ties and parading him naked in front of his family—during a mid-March attack in the occupied West Bank, according to the victim and eyewitnesses.

The Israeli military and police said the incident is under investigation. Israeli police reported that seven suspects had been arrested on suspicion of involvement, and that police officers and military personnel were dispatched to Humsah.

Suhaib Abu Kbash, 29, told Reuters that around 80 masked settlers armed with knives and sticks raided the Bedouin community of Humsah on March 13. He said about 20 of them beat him, stripped him, bound his genitals, and dragged him naked in front of his young children. "I thought they were going to kill me," he said.

Reuters spoke to three eyewitnesses who corroborated Abu Kbash's account.

Attacks by masked Jewish settlers, including the burning of cars and homes, beatings, and livestock theft, have become near-daily in the West Bank, according to Palestinians and rights groups tracking the violence.

The Yesha Council, which represents Jewish settlements, declined to comment.

Humsah sits between two hills in the Jordan Valley, an area that Israeli rights group B'Tselem has said faces daily settler attacks. The alleged assault on Abu Kbash was part of a pattern of escalating violence, the group's executive director Yuli Novak told Reuters.

With the fragile Israel-Hamas ceasefire holding, there has been a rise in settler violence against Palestinians in the West Bank, something human rights groups say the Israeli government turns a blind eye to.

Title: Iran War Live Updates: Trump Says U.S. Is Considering ‘Winding Down’ War
Author: The New York Times
Summary: President Trump indicated the U.S. is considering winding down its conflict with Iran, claiming progress near its objectives. Meanwhile, U.S. forces increased aerial assaults to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has threatened to close. The conflict has damaged energy infrastructure across nine countries, causing oil prices to rise. The U.S. temporarily eased sanctions on Iranian oil to address market impacts.
Content:
President Trump said in a social media post on Friday that the United States was considering “winding down” the war with Iran as it was “getting very close” to meeting its objectives. His remarks came even as U.S. officials said they were ramping up aerial assaults against Iranian drones and naval vessels in an effort to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Earlier, Mr. Trump told reporters on the White House lawn: “I don’t want to do a cease-fire. You know, you don’t do a cease-fire when you’re literally obliterating the other side.” The president has said multiple times that the war was nearly over, only for U.S. attacks to intensify.

As the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran approaches the three-week mark, American commanders are still trying to eliminate Iran’s ability to choke off the strait, the critical passageway in and out of the Persian Gulf. Iran has used a lethal combination of mines, missiles, and armed drones — or the threat of using them — to all but shut down shipping through the strait, through which passes a large part of the world’s oil and natural gas.

“I think we’ve won,” Mr. Trump said, referring to Iran, stating that “all they’re doing is clogging up the strait.” Later, in his social media post on Friday, he wrote: “The Hormuz Strait will have to be guarded and policed, as necessary, by other Nations who use it — The United States does not! If asked, we will help these Countries in their Hormuz efforts, but it shouldn’t be necessary once Iran’s threat is eradicated.”

Iran has continued to strike out at its Persian Gulf neighbors, shaking the world’s energy supplies and the global economy. At least 37 oil refineries, natural gas fields, and other energy sites in nine countries have been damaged in drone and missile attacks since the United States and Israel began bombarding Iran, a New York Times analysis found. U.S. and Middle Eastern officials have blamed those strikes on Iran, which has taken responsibility for some of them.

The price of oil rose once again on Friday, with the global benchmark crude settling around $112 a barrel, up more than 50 percent since the war began. The S&P 500 Index fell about 1.5 percent, losing ground for the fourth straight week.

The state-owned Kuwait Petroleum Corporation said a drone attack had caused fires at the Mina al-Ahmadi refinery for the second consecutive day. Israel reported launching targeted attacks on Tehran after Iranian missile fire set off sirens in Jerusalem and northern Israel overnight.

To help ease the surge in oil prices, the U.S. Treasury Department on Friday temporarily lifted sanctions on Iranian oil that is already loaded onto vessels at sea, authorizing it to be sold to most countries. The license applies to oil loaded on vessels as of March 20 and is extended until April 19.

Title: On a Holiday of Renewal, Iranians Are Mourning and Fearful
Author: Yeganeh Torbati and Arash Khamooshi
Summary: Iranians marked Nowruz under subdued conditions due to economic hardship, government violence, and recent U.S.-Israeli bombings. Traditionally a festive holiday, this year’s celebrations at Tehran’s Tajrish Bazaar were muted, with reduced crowds and somber mood amid ongoing mourning for victims of protests and conflict-related tolls.
Content:
People shopping for Nowruz, the Iranian new year holiday, which has been muted this year by the war, at Tajrish Bazaar in Tehran on Thursday.

Usually, in the days leading up to the Iranian new year, Tajrish Square in Tehran is an explosion of color and joyful clamor. Merchants and customers haggle over the flowers, tableware, and painted eggs that Iranians use to decorate their homes for the holiday, Nowruz. This year, though, the mood throughout the country is subdued, as Iranians close out a year in which they have endured deepening economic misery, a brutal massacre carried out by government forces, and U.S.-Israeli bombardment.

Nowruz, which falls on Friday and is believed to have been celebrated in ancient Iran since at least the fifth century B.C., marks the first day of spring and symbolizes new beginnings. Iranians traditionally set out tables adorned with objects representing renewal, health, and prosperity. They buy new clothes, spring clean their homes, and gather with friends. In significance and mood, it resembles a combination of Christmas, New Year’s Eve, and Independence Day.

This year, the holiday falls during the most difficult and perilous time in modern memory, giving Iranians little to celebrate. Thousands of families are still mourning loved ones killed by state forces during mass protests in January. Three weeks of U.S. and Israeli bombardment have killed civilians, destroyed homes, and put the nation on edge.

Crowds at Tajrish Bazaar on Thursday were noticeably reduced. Traditional goods like goldfish and colored eggs, which are sold for Nowruz, were available, but stalls did not have the usual crowds.

Title: Trump Organization Eyes Transylvania Golf Course Deal
Author: Rebecca R. Ruiz and Andrei Petre
Summary: The Trump Organization plans to develop luxury apartments and a golf course near a landfill in Cluj, Romania, marking its expansion into Transylvania. The project aims to revive a stalled development amid local issues, including Roma displacement. Despite previously pledging to halt international deals, the company continues global expansion through licensing, earning tens of millions in 2024.
Content:
The Trump Organization has long established its presence in major cities such as Manhattan, Los Angeles, and Honolulu, with ambitions extending to Australia, Bali, and the Maldives. Now, the family business is targeting Transylvania, the central Romanian region known for its association with Count Dracula. 

Records and interviews indicate that the company has selected a site near one of the region’s largest landfills and a former medical waste dump in the city of Cluj. The project, which has not yet been officially announced, aims to revive a stalled real estate development that was previously marred by government corruption. It would feature luxury apartments and a golf course close to an informal encampment where Roma people have been effectively exiled to live beside toxic waste.

The deal represents part of a broader global expansion of the Trump brand. During Donald Trump’s first presidential term, the company publicly pledged to cease new international deals, but this promise was not renewed after he returned to office. Instead, the firm has leveraged the president’s popularity in certain regions, particularly in Persian Gulf countries, through licensing agreements that allow use of the Trump name.

Financial disclosures suggest that these international licensing deals generated tens of millions of dollars in 2024. The company has announced additional projects and has also profited from ventures involving cryptocurrency.

Title: Dropdowns Inside Scrollable Containers: Why They Break And How To Fix Them Properly
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Godstime Aburu)
Summary: Dropdown menus inside scrollable containers often get clipped or hidden due to overflow settings (`overflow: hidden/auto`) and stacking context issues. Even with high z-index, menus may not appear correctly if their parent containers clip overflowing content. Understanding overflow behavior, positioning, and stacking contexts is essential to resolving these display problems.

Title: Modal vs. Separate Page: UX Decision Tree
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Vitaly Friedman)
Summary: Choosing between modals and pages impacts user flow, context, and task completion. Modals are suitable for self-contained tasks, warnings, or details without disrupting the current workflow, but they can be intrusive if misused. Proper differentiation among modals, overlays, dialogs, and lightboxes is crucial, with non-modals often being more user-friendly for subtle notifications.
Content:
How to choose between modals and pages, when to avoid modals, and how to determine the right level of interruption or navigation.

You probably have been there before. How do we choose between showing a modal to users, and when do we navigate them to a separate, new page? And does it matter at all?

Actually, it does. The decision influences users’ flow, their context, their ability to look up details, and with it error frequency and task completion. Both options can be disruptive and frustrating — at the wrong time, and at the wrong place.

So we’d better get it right. Well, let’s do just that.

Modals vs. Dialogs vs. Overlays vs. Lightboxes

While we often speak about a single modal UI component, we often ignore fine, intricate nuances between all the different types of modals. In fact, not every modal is the same. Modals, dialogs, overlays, and lightboxes — all sound similar, but they are actually quite different:

- **Dialog:** A generic term for “conversation” (user ↔ system).
- **Overlay:** A small content panel displayed on top of a page.
- **Modal:** User must interact with overlay + background disabled.
- **Nonmodal:** User must interact with overlay + background enabled.
- **Lightbox:** Dimmed background to focus attention on the modal.

As Anna Kaley highlights, most overlays appear at the wrong time, interrupt users during critical tasks, use poor language, and break users’ flow. They are interruptive by nature, and typically with a high level of severity without a strong need for that.

The many sides of modals and overlays. A little tree to understand the differences for UI components.

Surely users must be slowed down and interrupted if the consequences of their action have a high impact, but for most scenarios, non-modals are much more subtle and a more friendly option to bring something to the user’s attention. If anything, I always suggest it to be a default.

Modals → For Single, Self-Contained Tasks

As designers, we often dismiss modals as irrelevant and annoying — and often they are! — yet they have their value as well. They can be very helpful to warn users about potential mistakes or help them avoid data loss. They can also help perform related actions or drill down into details without interrupting the current state of the page.

But the biggest advantage of modals is that they help users keep the context of the current screen. It doesn’t mean just the UI, but also the user's ongoing task or workflow.

Title: Anime vs. Marvel/DC: Designing Digital Products With Emotion In Flow
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Alan Cohen)
Summary: The article compares anime like Dan da Dan and superhero films like James Gunn’s Superman to illustrate two emotional design approaches. "Emotion in Flow" ensures seamless emotional shifts through continuity and cues, exemplified by Dan da Dan. "Emotion in Conflict" involves jarring transitions seen in superhero movies. Applying these concepts, designers can create digital products that facilitate smooth emotional transitions and improve user engagement.
Content:
Design is about pacing and feelings as much as pixels and patterns. Alan Cohen explores Emotion in Flow and Emotion in Conflict, showing how anime like Dan Da Dan and superhero films like James Gunn’s Superman manage emotional shifts and translating those ideas into practical patterns for product design.

These aren’t only UX ideas; they are applicable across entertainment. The clearest way to feel the difference is to compare how anime handles emotional shifts versus how Marvel and DC films stumble. Using examples from Dan da Dan (an anime series on Netflix) and James Gunn’s Superman movie, the concepts are defined and then translated into practical product design patterns for digital products, including apps, SaaS, and web.

**Emotion In Flow (Anime: Dan da Dan)**

In Dan da Dan, the tonal range is wild—horror, comedy, tenderness—but it flows seamlessly. For example, in one arc, the protagonists are on a bizarre, comedic quest involving the “golden genitals” of a main character, and in another, viewers are drawn into a heartbreaking story of a mother whose child is kidnapped. On paper, these shifts could be jarring, but on screen, they are coherent and emotionally clear.

Why does this work? 

- **Continuity of stakes:** Even when humor is present, the characters’ goals and dangers remain consistent. Humor releases tension after a mini‑resolution without denying the threat.
- **Clear mood cues:** Music, framing, pacing, and character reactions signal upcoming emotional shifts, priming viewers to ride the change rather than be yanked.
- **An emotional anchor:** Relationships remain central, ensuring the scene’s core remains intact despite tonal shifts.

In UX, good products emulate this by preparing users for emotional transitions, facilitating smooth transitions, and resolving feelings to keep users immersed as their emotional tone shifts. Dan da Dan exemplifies this, transitioning flawlessly from humor to empathy.

**Emotion In Conflict (Marvel/DC: James Gunn’s Superman)**

In contrast, films like James Gunn’s Superman often undercut emotional moments with misplaced jokes, jarring transitions, or surprise pop-ups, disrupting the emotional flow. These moments create conflict rather than harmony, making the experience feel inconsistent and less immersive.

By understanding these two approaches—Emotion in Flow and Emotion in Conflict—designers can craft digital experiences that guide users through emotional shifts smoothly, enhancing engagement and clarity.

Title: NativePHP v3.1: The Biggest Performance Leap Yet
Author: NativePHP
Summary: Native PHP v3.1 introduces a major performance boost, reducing Laravel request times from 200-300ms to 5-30ms by booting Laravel once. It adds background job processing with dedicated threads, supports Android 8+ and full ICU on iOS, and includes features like PHP 8.3-8.5 auto-detection and plugin enhancements. Upgrading is straightforward with no breaking changes.
Content:
Native PHP v3.1 just dropped. And it’s not a minor bump. This is the single biggest performance update ever shipped, offering 10x faster response times, background job processing, Android 8+ support, full ICU on iOS, and more.

Every request used to go through a full Laravel boot cycle—200-300ms each time. That’s gone. V3.1 boots Laravel once and reuses the kernel across all subsequent requests, reducing response times to 5-30ms. Your app finally feels native. Livewire state, router state, facade instances—all handled automatically. It can fall back to classic mode if needed.

V3.1 ships with ZTS (Thread-Safe) PHP and a dedicated background queue worker on its own thread. Dispatch jobs like normal with QUEUE_CONNECTION=database. The worker starts automatically. Long-running tasks no longer block the UI. Jobs survive app restarts, supporting both iOS and Android.

Minimum SDK for Android drops from API 33 (Android 13) to API 26 (Android 8). More devices can now run your app. SDK versions are configurable: `'Android'=>['compile_sdk'=>env('NATIVEPHP_ANDROID_COMPILE_SDK',36),'min_sdk'=>env('NATIVEPHP_ANDROID_MIN_SDK',33),'target_sdk'=>env('NATIVEPHP_ANDROID_TARGET_SDK',36),],`

iOS builds now include full ICU support. The PHP intl extension works on both platforms. Filament works on iOS and Android out of the box, with no workarounds or compromises, optional via `--with-icu`/`--without-icu` flags.

Additional features include PHP 8.3-8.5 auto-detection from composer.json, local binary caching, plugin multi-register for discovering and registering all plugins in one pass, and platform shorthand—ios/andriod/everywhere. Unused dependencies like react/http and react/socket have been removed.

Upgrading is a drop-in process with no breaking changes. Use `composer require nativephp/mobile:~3.1.0` and `php artisan native:install --force`. Read the full announcement and update today.

Title: Prompt Deck: Manage AI Prompts as Versioned Files in Laravel
Author: Paul Redmond
Summary: Prompt Deck by Victor Ukam for Laravel manages AI prompts via versioned markdown files stored on disk, supporting variable interpolation, A/B testing, and version activation. Compatible with PHP 8.2+ and Laravel 11+, it offers artisan commands for prompt management and integrates with laravel/ai through HasPromptTemplate. The package facilitates structured prompt handling for AI applications.

Title: Laravel Prompts v0.3.15 Adds Streaming, Tasks, Autocomplete, and More
Author: Eric L. Barnes
Summary: Laravel Prompts v0.3.15 adds features for interactive CLI apps, including task() for long operations with live updates, stream() for incremental output, autocomplete() for suggestions, dynamic context panels in select prompts, title() for terminal titles, and notify() for system alerts. These tools enable building AI-powered, real-time, and user-friendly terminal applications.
Content:
Laravel Prompts v0.3.15 introduces several new features to facilitate the creation of interactive, real-time, and AI-driven terminal applications.

A new helper, task(), simplifies running long-running operations while keeping users informed. It includes a live-updating spinner, scrolling log output, and persistent success, warning, and error states that remain after completion. The task label can also be dynamically updated as progress changes, making it useful for deployments, installations, or processes requiring visibility.

The stream() prompt enables incremental output directly to the terminal, supporting word wrapping, ANSI support, and a smooth fade-in effect. This feature is ideal for displaying AI-generated responses, live logs, or progressive output from APIs—anything arriving over time can now be displayed naturally in the CLI.

With autocomplete(), users receive type-ahead suggestions while maintaining full control over input. Suggestions can be provided via a static array or a dynamic closure that filters options as the user types. This is useful for config keys, file paths, or frequently used values where speed is essential.

Select prompts now support dynamic context panels through the info parameter. By passing a closure, developers can display contextual details—such as pricing, descriptions, IDs, or metadata—based on the currently highlighted option across select, multiselect, search, multisearch, and suggest prompts.

The title() helper allows programmatic updates to the terminal window or tab title, which is especially helpful for long-running commands. Users can monitor progress at a glance, even when the terminal is in the background. Passing an empty string resets the title.

The notify() function brings native system notifications to CLI applications, supporting macOS (including subtitles and sounds) and Linux (with custom icons). This feature is useful for background tasks like deployments or builds, alerting users when processes complete.

These primitives can be combined to build AI-powered CLI applications. For example: accept user input, update the terminal title, process the request with an AI agent wrapped in task(), and stream the generated output with stream(). This pattern enables powerful AI-driven developer tools directly in the terminal.

Title: Arrests - Discharged Bear Spray - 400 Block Confederation Drive
Author: Unknown
Summary: A 12-year-old girl and a 14-year-old boy were arrested after discharging bear spray during a fight at a business on Confederation Drive. The girl was charged with weapon possession and concealment, while the boy faced charges of mischief, weapon possession, and concealment, with the boy also found with an imitation firearm.
Content:
A youth male and a youth female were arrested yesterday evening after bear spray was discharged in a business.

At around 7:15 p.m. on March 19, 2026, police were called to a business in the 400 block of Confederation Drive for a report of a large group of youth fighting and discharging bear spray. Upon arrival, officers were directed towards three youths who had not since fled; following continued investigation, two of the youths were arrested and found to be in possession of canisters of bear spray. One was also in possession of an imitation firearm.

As a result, a 12-year-old female was charged with possession of a weapon dangerous to public peace and unauthorized carry of a concealed weapon. A 14-year-old male was charged with mischief over $5000, possession of a weapon dangerous to public peace (x2), and unauthorized carry of a concealed weapon (x2).

Title: Unidentified Person - Request For Public Assistance
Author: Unknown
Summary: Saskatoon police and coroner’s service seek help identifying a deceased Caucasian male, aged 55-70, found outside on March 9, 2026. He had blond dyed hair, hazel eyes, and was dressed in black clothing. No ID was found, and his death is not considered suspicious. Contact police with information at 306-975-8300.
Content:
The Saskatoon Police Service Major Crime Section and Saskatchewan Coroner’s Service are requesting public assistance in identifying a person who was found deceased outside at around 6:00 a.m. on March 9, 2026, in the 100 block of 29th Street West.

The deceased is described as a Caucasian male between the ages of 55 and 70, with an average build and short dyed blond hair. He has hazel eyes, a small scar on the top of his nose, and is missing most teeth. The man’s clothing size is large, and he was found wearing a black H&M jacket with black framed glasses in the left pocket, black boots, a black zip-up hoodie, dark jeans, blue boxers, and a blue t-shirt. He also had a pair of sunglasses missing a lens in his possession. No identification was located on the deceased.

The circumstances of the man’s death are not believed to be suspicious in nature. Anyone with information that could assist with identifying the man is asked to contact police at 306-975-8300.

Title: Saskatoon Police Welcome Electronic Storage Detection Dog - Taffy
Author: Unknown
Summary: Taffy, a nearly three-year-old golden retriever, has joined the Saskatoon Police Service's ICE Unit after training in Indiana. She assists in locating electronic devices during investigations and provides therapy support for crime victims and officers. Taffy was donated by OUR Rescue and is the first ESD dog in Saskatchewan, enhancing efforts against child exploitation.
Content:
Meet Taffy! Recently, the Saskatoon Police Service welcomed Taffy to the Saskatchewan Internet Child Exploitation (ICE) Unit following her successful completion of the Jordan Detection K9 Electronic Storage Detection (ESD) program in Indianapolis, Indiana, with 22 other dog teams. Taffy is a nearly three-year-old golden retriever who began her work with the SPS earlier this year alongside her handler, Detective Sergeant Bryon Sommacal.

In addition to her primary work to locate electronic devices during the execution of search warrants, Taffy also serves a secondary role as a deep therapy support dog for victims of crime and members of the SPS. Since beginning her work, Taffy has fulfilled each function of her role; locating hidden electronic devices during the execution of search warrants, providing comfort to a child victim of a sex crime, and, most recently, supporting members of the SPS following the sudden loss of a colleague.

The SPS obtained Taffy at no cost through OUR Rescue, a non-profit organization dedicated to supporting law enforcement agencies in efforts to combat human trafficking and child exploitation through the donation of ESD dogs and costs associated with training and annual recertifications. Taffy is one of eight dog teams in Canada and the first in Saskatchewan trained in locating electronic devices in internet child exploitation investigations.

ESD dogs are purpose-trained to detect the chemical compound commonly found in electronic devices such as cell phones, USBs, hard drives, SD cards, laptops, and other digital media that may contain evidence relevant to an internet child exploitation investigation.

“Digital evidence plays a significant role in internet child exploitation investigations,” says Staff Sergeant Tim Failler, Coordinator of the Saskatchewan ICE Unit. “Even the smallest device can hold thousands of images, videos, and messages. The introduction of Taffy as a resource for investigators greatly increases our ability to locate those devices and hold offenders accountable.”

The addition of Taffy at the SPS to support the work of the Saskatchewan ICE Unit furthers our commitment to providing effective response and quality service to protect our community and meet their needs.

Title: Iranian strikes on bases used by US caused $800m in damage, new analysis shows
Author: Unknown
Summary: Iranian strikes on US military bases in the Middle East caused approximately $800 million in damages within two weeks, mainly targeting air-defense and communication systems. The damage includes $485 million to a radar system in Jordan and $310 million to infrastructure. Iran has repeatedly targeted bases like Ali Al-Salim, Al-Udeid, and Prince Sultan. Since the conflict began, 13 US service members have died, with total casualties around 3,200, including civilians.
Content:
Iranian strikes on military bases used by the US in the Middle East caused about $800 million (£600 million) in damage in the first two weeks of the war, a new analysis shows.

Much of the damage was caused in initial retaliatory strikes by Iran in the week after the US and Israel launched the war, according to a report by the Center for Strategic & International Studies (CSIS) and an analysis by the BBC. The full extent of the damage caused by Iranian strikes on US assets in the region is not clear. However, the $800 million in estimated damages to US military infrastructure—higher than previously reported—offers a picture of the steep costs to the US as the conflict drags on.

"The damage to US bases in the region has been underreported," said Mark Cancian, a CSIS senior adviser and co-author of the think tank study. "Although that appears to be extensive, the full amount won't be known until more information is available."

In response to a request for comment, the US Department of Defense referred the BBC to US Central Command, which is leading the war. Officials there declined to comment.

Iran's retaliatory strikes targeted US air-defense and satellite-communication systems, among other assets, in Jordan, the United Arab Emirates, and other countries across the Middle East. A significant portion of damage was caused by a strike on a US radar for a Thaad missile defense system at an air base in Jordan. The AN/TPY-2 radar system costs approximately $485 million, according to a CSIS review of defense department budget documents. These air-defense systems are used for the long-range interception of ballistic missiles.

Strikes by Iran caused an additional $310 million in estimated damages to buildings, facilities, and other infrastructure on US bases and military bases used by American forces in the region. Iran has also struck at least three air bases more than once, according to satellite imagery analyzed by BBC Verify. The repeat strikes underscore Iran's efforts to target specific US assets. Russia has reportedly shared intelligence with Tehran on American military forces in the region.

Satellite imagery shows the three air bases—Ali Al-Salim in Kuwait, Al-Udeid in Qatar, and Prince Sultan in Saudi Arabia—with fresh damage appearing during different phases of the conflict.

Since President Donald Trump joined Israel in launching attacks on Iran on February 28, the US has lost 13 military service members. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (Hrana) estimates the overall death toll has reached nearly 3,200, including 1,400 civilians.

Title: US lifts sanctions on some Iranian oil as gas prices soar
Author: Unknown
Summary: The US lifted sanctions on some Iranian oil, allowing limited sales until April 19, potentially releasing 140 million barrels to global markets. The move aims to address soaring gas prices but is unlikely to significantly impact prices and may increase funds for Iran. Experts criticized the policy, citing concerns over funding Iran's regime and limited market effects.
Content:
The US has lifted sanctions on some Iranian oil as gas prices soar. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced the issuance of a narrowly tailored, short-term authorization permitting the sale of Iranian oil currently stranded at sea. The move marks a significant reversal of longstanding American policy and carries highly uncertain outcomes.

Experts indicated that the measure is likely to have a limited effect on prices and could potentially increase funds flowing to the Iranian regime. Globally, energy prices are rising as the war impacts shipping and production. On Friday, Bessent stated that the permit applies to the sale of crude oil and petroleum products of Iranian origin currently loaded on vessels. The authorization will last until April 19, and the Treasury Department estimates it could bring about 140 million barrels of oil to global markets.

Before the conflict, China was the primary buyer of Iranian oil, purchasing barrels at a steep discount due to sanctions. In an interview with Fox Business, Bessent suggested that a waiver on sales restrictions could help redirect supplies to countries like India, Japan, and Malaysia, while forcing China to pay market prices. However, he did not detail how the waiver would function or whether it would include rules to prevent proceeds from returning to the Iranian government.

David Tannenbaum, director of Blackstone Compliance Services, criticized the move, telling the BBC, "Essentially we're allowing Iran to sell oil, which could then be used to fund the war effort." President Donald Trump was asked about the proposal but did not provide a clear stance, stating only that the US would do "whatever is necessary to keep the price."

Experts warned that the waiver would likely have minimal impact on prices. Rachel Ziemba, an adjunct senior fellow at the Center for a New American Security, said, "I don't think it's a game changer and it raises a whole lot of questions." She noted that while the US may not want money from Iranian oil sales, preventing funds from flowing back to Iran could be difficult in practice, especially given the current supply shocks.

The US has already taken other measures to increase supply, including releasing millions of barrels from reserves and suspending some sanctions on Russian oil last week. This latter decision drew criticism from European leaders, who argued it would strengthen Vladimir Putin's regime and prolong the conflict in Ukraine.

Title: Norway's crown princess breaks silence, claiming she was 'manipulated and deceived' by Epstein
Author: Unknown
Summary: Norwegian Crown Princess Mette-Marit expressed regret over her contacts with Jeffrey Epstein, stating she wishes she had never met him. She revealed exchanging emails and staying at his Florida home between 2011-2014, claiming she was manipulated and unaware of his crimes. She apologized for poor judgment, emphasized her lack of knowledge about his abuse, and condemned Epstein’s victims.
Content:
Norway's Crown Princess Mette-Marit has told national TV that she wishes she had never met late US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, breaking seven weeks of silence after the extent of her contacts with him emerged.

"I feel so manipulated, and when you are manipulated, you don't realise it from the start," Mette-Marit said in a 20-minute interview in which she was often on the verge of tears.

Seven weeks ago, Norwegians discovered that the crown princess had exchanged hundreds of emails with the disgraced Epstein between 2011 and 2014, and stayed in his Florida house when he was not there.

"It is incredibly important for me to take responsibility for not checking his background more carefully," she said. "And to take responsibility for being so manipulated and deceived as I was."

She has already apologized and admitted to "poor judgement" after the close nature of her links to Epstein came to light when millions of Epstein files were released by the US justice department at the end of January.

"Of course I wish I had never met him," the princess told public broadcaster NRK, stressing that it was Epstein's victims who deserved justice for the great abuse they had suffered. She said she felt great anger that they had not yet received it.

Her decision to speak publicly comes after intense scrutiny and pressure to explain herself, including from Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre.

The crown princess gave little away during the conversation, and some of her responses came across as defensive. In 2011, three years after Epstein had been jailed for soliciting underage sex, she wrote: "Googled u after last email. Agree didn't look too good."

Sitting alongside her husband, Crown Prince Haakon, during the interview, Mette-Marit maintained she "didn't know he was a sex offender or a predator," even though the reporter pointed out that a Wikipedia article on Epstein at the time had made clear he was a convicted abuser.

"I can't remember this; it was 15 years ago."

The crown princess described phoning her husband after an "uneasy" incident during her stay at Epstein's Florida home.

"I still didn't know anything about all the abuse. But I had understood enough that I thought he was a bad guy who people shouldn't have contact with," she told NRK. "And I had up close how he blackmailed others. So I regret that I didn't tell more people, because I should have."

She admitted to being too trusting of Epstein, but when asked why neither the palace nor the foreign ministry knew about her links to him, she said he was a "private contact" and she did not tell everyone about her private contacts.

Asked why she spent several days in Epstein's home in Palm Beach in 2013, she explained that it was down to an unnamed mutual acquaintance. "Epstein was a close friend of a good friend of mine," she said.

Title: Elon Musk misled Twitter investors while trying to get out of acquisition, jury says
Author: Amanda Silberling
Summary: A California jury found Elon Musk intentionally misled Twitter investors by tweeting false concerns about bots to back out of his $44 billion Twitter deal in 2022. The lawsuit, filed by shareholder Giuseppe Pampena, claims Musk's actions caused stock declines and losses. Damages could reach $2.6 billion, though Musk's net worth exceeds $660 billion.
Content:
A civil jury in California ruled that Elon Musk intentionally misled Twitter investors when he tried to back out of his $44 billion acquisition of the platform in 2022. At the time, Musk had tweeted that Twitter had too many bots, which he later cited as a reason to renegotiate the deal. Twitter sued Musk to force him to complete the purchase.

Musk posted on Twitter that the deal was temporarily on hold pending details supporting the claim that spam and fake accounts represented less than 5% of users. Following this tweet, Twitter shares declined by 8%. Investor Giuseppe Pampena filed suit against Musk on behalf of other former Twitter shareholders who sold their shares between May 13, 2022, and October 4, 2022, when the deal was finalized.

Pampena’s lawsuit argued that Musk intentionally posted concerns about Twitter’s user base to create uncertainty and artificially lower its stock price, causing shareholders who sold during that period to suffer losses. Musk’s attorneys contended that he was expressing legitimate concerns about the number of bots on the platform. However, the jury was convinced by the plaintiff’s argument.

It is not yet clear how much Musk will have to pay in damages, but Pampena’s attorney estimated that damages could reach up to $2.6 billion. This would be a relatively small amount for Musk, whose net worth is estimated at over $660 billion.

This is not Musk’s first legal issue related to tweets. In 2018, he tweeted that he had secured funding to take Tesla private at $420 per share, which the SEC alleged was misleading and charged Musk with securities fraud. Musk testified that he believed he would take Tesla private at that price, which represented a significant premium over the stock’s current value.

Title: Microsoft rolls back some of its Copilot AI bloat on Windows
Author: Sarah Perez
Summary: Microsoft is reducing AI integration in Windows 11, notably limiting Copilot in apps like Photos and Notepad, to focus on meaningful AI experiences. This “less-is-more” approach aims to address consumer concerns about AI trust and safety. The company has also delayed some AI features, such as Windows Recall, and is implementing other user-focused updates based on feedback.
Content:
Microsoft announced a series of changes focused on improving the quality of its Windows 11 operating system, notably including reducing the number of entry points to its AI assistant, Copilot. The company said it will decrease Copilot AI integrations in some apps, starting with Photos, Widgets, Notepad, and the Snipping Tool.

Under the heading of “integrating AI where it’s most meaningful,” Pavan Davuluri, EVP of Windows and Devices, stated on the company’s blog that Microsoft is becoming more intentional about “how and where Copilot integrates across Windows.” Its goal is to focus on AI experiences that are “genuinely useful.”

This “less-is-more” approach to AI integration may reflect growing consumer pushback against AI bloat. While many recognize AI as a useful tool, there are concerns around trust and safety. A Pew Research study published this month noted that half of U.S. adults are now more concerned than excited about AI as of June 2025, up from 37% in 2021.

Microsoft has previously reconsidered its Copilot integrations. Earlier this month, the news site Windows Central reported that the company’s plan to ship Copilot-branded AI features across Windows 11 had been quietly shelved. This included some system-level integrations within the Settings app, File Explorer, and elsewhere.

Additionally, Microsoft delayed the launch of its AI-powered memory feature, Windows Recall for Copilot + PCs, for over a year to address privacy concerns. Although the Recall feature launched last April, security vulnerabilities are still being discovered.

User feedback has influenced Microsoft’s AI-related decisions on Windows. Davuluri mentioned that he and his team have spent months listening to the community about how they’d like to see improvements.

The Copilot rollback is just one of several changes. The company also announced the ability to move the taskbar to the top or sides of the screen, more control over system updates, faster File Explorer, improved Widgets experience, updates to the Feedback Hub, and easier navigation of the Windows Insider Program.

Title: What happened at Nvidia GTC: NemoClaw, Robot Olaf, and a $1 trillion bet
Author: Theresa Loconsolo
Summary: At Nvidia’s GTC conference, CEO Jensen Huang announced a $1 trillion AI chip sales goal by 2027, emphasizing an "Open Claw strategy" and showcasing innovations like Nemo Claw and Robot Olaf. The event highlighted Nvidia’s aim to dominate AI infrastructure across various industries, impacting startups and broader tech development.
Content:
Startups

What happened at Nvidia GTC: Nemo Claw, Robot Olaf, and a $1 trillion bet

Theresa Loconsolo

CEO Jensen Huang took the stage at Nvidia’s GTC conference this week in his signature leather jacket to deliver a two-and-a-half-hour keynote, projecting $1 trillion in AI chip sales through 2027, declaring that every company needs an “Open Claw strategy,” and closing with a rambling Olaf robot that had to get its mic cut. The message was clear: Nvidia aims to be foundational to everything, from AI training to autonomous vehicles to Disney parks.

On this episode of Tech Crunch’s Equity podcast, Kirsten Korosec, Anthony Ha, and Sean O’Kane analyze what Nvidia’s expanding web of AI infrastructure partnerships means for startups, and discuss other headlines from the week.

Theresa Loconsolo is an audio producer at Tech Crunch focusing on Equity, the network’s flagship podcast. Before joining Tech Crunch in 2022, she was one of two producers at a four-station conglomerate, where she wrote, recorded, voiced, and edited content, and engineered live performances and interviews with guests like lovelytheband. Theresa is based in New Jersey and holds a bachelor’s degree in Communication from Monmouth University.

Title: Crimson Desert interactive maps
Author: Polygon Staff
Summary: Crimson Desert's Pywel continent is vast and challenging to navigate without guidance. Interactive maps help players find key locations, quests, vendors, and resources. Guides assist newcomers in understanding game systems and developing skills like fishing and mining. Walkthroughs cover main story quests and Abyss puzzles, emphasizing the importance of exploration and resource collection for progression.
Content:
Crimson Desert's continent of Pywel is a large area, and it's easy to miss many of the game's best features if you're not sure where to look. The game often doesn't provide much guidance on how to find fast travel points or locate special abyss artifacts. While some discovery is part of the experience, falling behind on collecting abyss artifacts and upgrading weapons can have noticeable consequences.

Interactive maps are available to help players find everything important, including faction quests, inns, vendors—both legitimate and shady back-alley ones—bounty boards, dungeons, caves, resource nodes like iron ore and abyss cressets, and prime fishing spots.

For newcomers, there are guides to learn the basics and refresh knowledge of game systems. The world of Pywel is vast, making it challenging to decide where to go first. Players are encouraged to develop life skills such as fishing, logging, cooking, and mining, which can be profitable.

Additionally, walkthroughs are provided for main story quests like "Abyss Without Balance" and "Familiar Curses," as well as puzzles in the Abyss such as Root's End.

Title: Outfits and how to unlock them in Pokémon Pokopia
Author: Julia Lee
Summary: In Pokémon Pokopia, players can customize Ditto with outfits and hairstyles found as "human records" across the island, including magazines, tablets, and newspapers. Key outfits include Team Rocket, Rock-solid, Ranger, Ninja, Psychic Master, Black Belt, Sailor, and Super Nerd, located in various areas such as Sparkling Skylands, Rocky Ridges, Bleak Beach, and Withered Wasteland. Some cosmetics are found on main islands, others on randomized Dream Islands.
Content:
This article is part of a directory: Pokémon Pokopia guides and walkthroughs.

If you want to customize your Ditto in Pokémon Pokopia, you can find outfits and hairstyles around the deserted island to swap in. Many of these outfits are based on gym leaders and trainers from previous games. All cosmetics are found as "human records" scattered around the island, appearing as magazines, tablets, books, or newspapers with a yellow sparkle on the ground. Interacting with them adds these outfits to your collection. Once unlocked, you can use the pieces separately to mix and match, such as combining a Team Rocket hat with a Super Nerd button-up. Interact with a mirror to swap your look.

Some cosmetics are located in set spots on the main island, while others can only be found on randomized Dream Islands. The following details where to find various outfits and hairstyles in Pokémon Pokopia.

There are a few cosmetics available across the four main areas in Pokopia:

- **Team Rocket outfit:** Located on the northwest island in Sparkling Skylands. Enter the building from the top and climb down the metal stairs.
- **Rock-solid outfit:** Found in the mines from the entrance where DJ Rotom is met in Rocky Ridges. Proceed through the lava-filled room, drop through the opening in the middle, and continue forward. Use the ladders to reach the book on the floor.
- **Ranger outfit:** Located next to the furnace in the industrial ruins at Bleak Beach.
- **Ninja outfit:** Inside a ruined home east of the Withered Wasteland Pokémon Center.
- **Psychic Master outfit:** On the eastern island of Sparkling Skylands, on the floor of the eroded gym.
- **Black Belt outfit:** On the ground of the dojo in Sparkling Skylands, accessible via the elevator on the northern island.
- **Sailor outfit:** On the S.S. Anne in Bleak Beach, on the bridge near the steering wheel.
- **Super Nerd outfit:** On the path into the mines from the room where DJ Rotom is first encountered in Rocky Ridges.
- **Parted hair:** Inside the cave where you free Onix in the Withered Wasteland.
- **Mermaid hair:** On top of a cliff overlooking the ruined town north of the Pokémon Center in Withered Wasteland.

Title: Crimson Desert guides and walkthroughs
Author: Polygon Staff
Summary: Crimson Desert is an open-world medieval game set in Pywel, featuring quests, puzzles, battles, and exploration. Players start as Kliff, gathering friends while navigating a vast, maze-like world with limited tutorials. Key tips include choosing skills, respecs, fast travel, and resource gathering. Guides and maps assist with navigation, puzzle solutions, and quest completion.
Content:
Crimson Desert is a vast open-world game that takes you and your Greymane buddies through Pywel, a medieval realm full of knights, bandits, witches, and even cats. You'll start off as Kliff, who is looking to get the gang back together, but you'll need to take a pit stop in Hernand first. As you meet up with your friends, you'll encounter numerous puzzles both on land and in the Abyss, take on difficult battles, and perhaps pet a dog or two.

Pywel is huge and extremely easy to get lost in. Everyone will have a different playthrough due to the world's size and options, but that can also be daunting. To help you get started, a guide and interactive map are available to help you become familiar with the world. If you want a sense of direction, these resources can guide you on where to go first.

Crimson Desert is fairly hands-off with its tutorials, which can make its quests and puzzles a little difficult to solve. Right from the beginning, you'll be thrown into the Abyss and unraveling ancient ruins, but they're no easy task. Before you set off, you should gear up, stock up on food, and choose the best skills for your playstyle. If you'd rather explore, look out for Abyss cressets and Abyss nexuses to traverse the world quickly.

Getting started tips include knowing where to go first, understanding the best skills to level up, how to respec, how to make money, and how to fast travel. Additionally, players can learn when they get a house in Crimson Desert, complete quest walkthroughs such as "Abyss Without Balance," "Reunion," "Familiar Curses," and "Bustling Hill." Puzzle solutions are available for challenges like the Dragon's Stone Chamber, Root's End Abyss, and the Precipice of Truth Abyss.

Resources can be collected through fishing, mining ore, cutting trees, stealing, and pickpocketing. To power up, players can craft weapons and armor, cook, and obtain palmar pills. Locations of witches are also part of the exploration.

Title: The Pout-Pout Fish
Author: Unknown
Summary: "The Pout-Pout Fish" (2026) is a PG animated family film set for release on March 20, 2026, directed by Ricard Cussó and Rio Harrington. Featuring a cast including Nick Offerman and Miranda Otto, it follows two aquatic misfits on a quest to save their home. Based on a bestselling children’s book series by Deborah Diesen, published by Macmillan.
Content:
The Pout-Pout Fish (2026) is an animated family film rated PG, with a runtime of 92 minutes. It is scheduled for release on March 20, 2026. The film is directed by Ricard Cussó and Rio Harrington, written by Elise Allen, Elie Choufany, and Dominic Morris. The cast includes Nick Offerman, Nina Oyama, Miranda Otto, Remy Hii, Jordin Sparks, and Amy Sedaris. The studio behind the film is Viva Pictures.

The story follows two aquatic misfits on an impossible journey to save their home. It is based on the New York Times bestselling children’s book series written by Deborah Diesen and illustrated by Dan Hanna. Published by Macmillan Children’s Publishing Group, the series has sold over 10 million copies and has been translated into numerous languages. It was named a Top Ten Children’s Book by TIME Magazine.

Title: Project Hail Mary
Author: Unknown
Summary: "Project Hail Mary" (2026) is a PG-13 sci-fi film directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, starring Ryan Gosling. It follows Ryland Grace, a scientist waking on a spaceship with amnesia, tasked with saving Earth from a dying sun. Based on Andy Weir's novel, the film explores his efforts to solve the mystery and includes an unexpected friendship.
Content:
Project Hail Mary (2026) is a science fiction drama action film rated PG-13, with a runtime of 156 minutes. It is directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, written by Drew Goodard, and stars Ryan Gosling, Sandra Hüller, Lionel Boyce, Ken Leung, and Milana Vayntrub. The studio is MGM.

The story follows science teacher Ryland Grace, who wakes up on a spaceship light years from Earth with no memory of who he is or how he got there. As his memory returns, he uncovers his mission: to solve the mystery of a substance causing the sun to die out. Using his scientific knowledge and unorthodox ideas, he must find a way to save Earth from extinction. An unexpected friendship complicates his mission.

"Project Hail Mary" is an adaptation of Andy Weir's popular science fiction novel, offering an entertaining and relatively faithful rendition of the story.

Title: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come
Author: Unknown
Summary: After surviving an attack from the Le Domas family, Grace (Samara Weaving) learns she has advanced in the deadly game, now with her sister Faith (Kathryn Newton). She must survive, protect her sister, and win the throne from four rival families vying for control of the world.
Content:
Moments after surviving an all-out attack from the Le Domas family, Grace (Samara Weaving) discovers she’s reached the next level of the nightmarish game — and this time with her estranged sister Faith (Kathryn Newton) at her side. Grace has one chance to survive, keep her sister alive, and claim the High Seat of the Council that controls the world.

Four rival families are hunting her for the throne, and whoever wins rules it all.

Title: ‘Baywatch’ Adds Nadia Gray to Recurring Cast
Author: Varietymattdonnelly
Summary: Nadia Gray joins Fox’s “Baywatch” reboot as Lisa, an EMT and lifeguard partner. She appears alongside stars including Stephen Amell, Brooks Nader, and others. Gray has recent roles in “Paradise Disturbed,” “All Rise,” “Bright,” and “Days of Our Lives.” Production is underway in Los Angeles, with Amell starring as Hobie Buchannon.
Content:
Fox’s highly anticipated “Baywatch” reboot has added Nadia Gray as a recurring guest star, Variety has confirmed. Gray will play Lisa, an EMT and partner to the team of lifeguards on Venice Beach. The character is said to have a particular affinity for one person on the Baywatch squad. 

Gray joins a previously announced ensemble that includes Stephen Amell, Brooks Nader, Hassie Harrison, Shay Mitchell, Jessica Belkin, Thaddeus La Grone, Livvy Dunne, and Noah Beck. She will next appear in a supporting lead role in the indie film “Paradise Disturbed,” opposite Holt Mc Callany (“The Iron Claw”). Her previous roles include the CBS series “All Rise,” alongside Wilson Bethel, a supporting role in the Netflix film “Bright” starring Will Smith and Joel Edgerton, and a recurring part on NBC’s “Days of our Lives.”

Gray is represented by Adrian Hamerski at Brave Artists Management and lawyer Adam Vitabile at Hirsch Wallerstein Hayum Matlof and Fishman. Other recurring stars booked for the new “Baywatch” include Ashley Moore, Kylar Miranda, Luke Eisner, and Charlie McElveen. Production in Los Angeles has attracted paparazzi attention during its early days.

Amell will star as Hobie Buchannon, described as the “wild child we all loved from the original series,” who “is now a Baywatch Captain, following in the footsteps of his legendary father, Mitch.” The official synopsis states, “Hobie’s world is turned upside down when Charlie, the daughter he never knew, shows up on his doorstep, eager to carry on the Buchannon family legacy and become a Baywatch lifeguard alongside her dad.”

Title: SXSW 2026: The 8 Best Things We Watched in Austin, From a Semi-Suicidal Charlie Day to a Talking Fox Played by Olivia Colman
Author: Kate Aurthur
Summary: The 2024 SXSW Film & TV Festival featured diverse titles, including headliners like “I Love Boosters,” “Over Your Dead Body,” and “They Will Kill You.” The final season of “The Comeback,” starring Lisa Kudrow, premiered after a decade, with its third season airing on HBO on March 22. Notable documentaries included Netflix’s “Noah Kahan: Out of Body” and other indie projects seeking distribution.
Content:
Though this year’s South by Southwest Film & TV Festival was an abbreviated, seven-day affair, filmmakers still brought Austin’s crowds an assortment of film and TV titles full of blood, laughter, and tears to complement their tacos and barbecue. It was a strong year for the SXSW headliners section, which is populated with studio-backed titles and celebrity names. Three of them made the best-of-fest list: Boots Riley’s Keke Palmer-led sci-fi comedy “I Love Boosters,” Jorma Taccone’s comedic thriller “Over Your Dead Body” starring Jason Segel and Samara Weaving, and the horror comedy “They Will Kill You” from Kirill Sokolov, starring Zazie Beetz as its murderous heroine. SXSW is known for its love of gory humor.

SXSW officially added TV to its lineup a few years back, and no series on the 2026 lineup justified that move more than “The Comeback.” Created by Michael Patrick King and Lisa Kudrow, with Kudrow starring as a washed-up reality TV star, the world premiere of the third and final season after a decade-long hiatus was a welcome presence at the festival.

On the documentary front, the standout was Netflix’s “Noah Kahan: Out of Body,” which is soon to premiere on Netflix. Other notable picks include “The Fox,” “Kill Me,” and “Their Town,” all of which are smartly written indie projects still seeking distribution.

It’s been 11 and a half years since “The Comeback” last aired, and for fans, that’s far too long. The first two episodes of Season 3, which premiered at the Paramount Theatre on March 16, felt worth the wait. “The Comeback,” Lisa Kudrow and Michael Patrick King’s brilliant creation, is like a comet that appears every ten years or so, and this time, it will circle the Earth just once more when its third season premieres on HBO on March 22.

The show centers on Kudrow’s Valerie Cherish—a yearning sitcom star who’s never quite achieved her dreams—who finds herself in a state of duress. This time, she’s been offered the leading role in the first TV comedy fully written by AI. Valerie worries about this, just a little, but her ambition quickly takes over as she dives into the opportunity.

Title: Crisis at Row K: New Indie Film Distributor’s Money Problems Put Reputations and Maude Apatow’s Directorial Debut in Jeopardy (EXCLUSIVE)
Author: Varietymattdonnelly
Summary: Row K Entertainment, a new Hollywood distribution label launched less than eight months ago, faces financial difficulties, including unpaid bills and work stoppages. Its slate, including films like “Poetic License” and “Dead Man’s Wire,” has been delayed or affected. Founders Christopher Woodrow and Raj Singh are central to the issues, with concerns about cash flow and strategic direction.
Content:
Less than eight months after it launched, Row K Entertainment, a new Hollywood distribution label, is being beset with unpaid bills and concerns about its finances. Eight sources from outside the company and within told Variety that cash flow has been an increasingly urgent issue at Row K since its inception last summer. Vendors and consultants haven’t been paid for months, resulting in work stoppages and legal threats.

After a splashy debut at the 2025 Toronto International Film Festival, where the company acquired four movies, Row K’s slate looks imperiled. One of the films that Row K picked up during TIFF, Maude Apatow’s directorial debut “Poetic License,” has been pushed from a planned May release to September. The decision has baffled Apatow’s team and inner circle, including the film’s producer, Judd Apatow, and its star, Leslie Mann. The film earned glowing reviews when it debuted at TIFF and screened at SXSW. Had it not moved, Maude Apatow would have released the film at the same time she will star in the anticipated third season of “Euphoria.”

Other titles on the slate include January’s “Dead Man’s Wire” from Gus Van Sant and the romantic indie “Charlie Harper.” At the center of the storm are Row K founders Christopher Woodrow and Raj Singh. Woodrow, a producer with credits including Alejandro González Iñárritu’s “Birdman,” was involved in a contentious 2014 lawsuit with backers from his former company, Worldview Entertainment, which claimed he embezzled and mismanaged funds. In 2018, Woodrow and Worldview settled the litigation without admitting liability. Singh has a smaller profile in the industry but formerly served as CEO of the comedy shop National Lampoon.

Row K’s release of “Dead Man’s Wire” has provided an opportunity to evaluate and refine their strategy as a new entrant in a highly competitive marketplace. The company states, “Like many early-stage companies, we moved quickly to establish a slate and are now taking a more measured, disciplined approach—prioritizing commercially viable titles.”

Title: Jury finds Musk owes damages to Twitter investors for his tweets
Author: John Timmer
Summary: A California jury found Elon Musk misled investors through public statements about Twitter, causing its stock to decline before his purchase. The class action lawsuit could result in Musk owing up to $2.6 billion in damages. The jury rejected claims of a larger scheme but held Musk liable for the misleading tweets.
Content:
On Friday, a jury in California determined that Elon Musk misled investors in Twitter via public statements that depressed the price of the company's stock ahead of his ultimately successful purchase of the service. Because this was a class action lawsuit, Musk is likely to owe damages to a large number of investors—payments that may reach billions of dollars.

In the lead-up to Musk’s purchase of the social media platform, he made several comments on Twitter and while appearing as a guest on a podcast, largely focused on the alleged prevalence of bot accounts on the platform. These statements raised fears that the deal might not go through and caused Twitter’s share price to decline, leading some investors to sell shares at a depressed price during this period.

A number of those investors initiated a suit that was certified as a class action, claiming that Musk's statements defrauded them and that he made them intentionally as part of a larger scheme. The jury rejected arguments about this larger scheme but found Musk liable for the tweets. While damages have yet to be determined, lawyers for the plaintiffs reportedly say they could reach as high as $2.6 billion.

Title: You're likely already infected with a brain-eating virus you've never heard of
Author: Beth Mole
Summary: Researchers warn that the JC virus, infecting up to 90% of people silently, can activate and cause progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), a deadly brain disease. PML destroys myelin in the brain, leading to neurological deficits. While rare, cases have increased, especially among immunocompromised individuals, though treatments have improved survival rates.
Content:
There’s a virus you may have never heard of before that is estimated to infect up to 90 percent of people and lurks quietly in your cells for life—but if it becomes activated, it will destroy your brain. Researchers reported this week that there may be a new way for this virus to activate—one that affects up to 10 percent of adults worldwide.

The virus is the human polyomavirus 2, commonly called either the JC virus or John Cunningham virus, named after the patient from whom it was first isolated in 1971. It shows up in the urine and stool of infected people and spreads via the fecal-oral route. Many people are thought to be infected early in life, and blood testing surveys have suggested that 50–90 percent of adults have been exposed at some point.

Researchers hypothesize that the initial site of infection is the tonsils, or perhaps the gastrointestinal tract. But wherever it happens, that initial infection is asymptomatic. At that point, a person is infected with what’s called the archetype JC virus, which quietly sets up a persistent but utterly silent lifelong infection.

For the vast majority of people, that is all their JC virus infection will be—silent. But for an unlucky few, the JC virus will awaken, rearrange its genetic material, and morph into a brain-demolishing disease called progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy or PML.

Devastating disease

In PML, the new disease-causing virus or “PML-type” JC virus actively invades the brain, destroying specific brain cells, including those that form the insulating myelin sheaths that protect nerve cells. This leads to extensive demyelination, resulting in nerve cell dysfunction and death. On imaging, PML can show up as signature lesions in the brain. These lesions, paired with test findings of JC virus DNA in cerebrospinal fluid, are used to diagnose PML. Symptoms can mimic everything from a stroke to multiple sclerosis, causing problems like speech impairments, visual defects, motor dysfunction, and seizures.

PML was first identified in 1958 in a cancer patient. It was considered an extremely rare condition until the 1980s, when it started to appear in patients with HIV/AIDS. In fact, PML became an AIDS-defining disease, with 2–5 percent of HIV-infected patients developing it in the early phase of the epidemic. At that point, the condition was uniformly fatal. But with the introduction of highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) in 1996, PML cases declined, and the disease was no longer a death sentence, though survivors often have significant lasting damage.

Expanding cases

In the decades since its discovery, PML has been considered a disease that strikes amid profound immune suppression. It’s thought that the JC virus can activate and rearrange.

Title: Once again, ULA can't deliver when the US military needs a satellite in orbit
Author: Stephen Clark
Summary: The US Space Force is shifting its GPS satellite launches from ULA’s Vulcan rocket to SpaceX’s Falcon 9 due to repeated technical issues with Vulcan’s boosters. The final GPS Block III satellite will now launch on Falcon 9 in April, marking the fourth such transfer in recent months. ULA’s Vulcan remains grounded pending investigation results.
Content:
For the fourth time in a little more than a year, the US Space Force needs to send up a new satellite to replenish the military’s GPS navigation network. And once again, the company the Pentagon is paying to launch it can’t answer the call.

United Launch Alliance, a 50-50 joint venture between Boeing and Lockheed Martin, was supposed to launch the final satellite for the Space Force’s GPS Block III program this month. Space Systems Command, responsible for buying spacecraft and rockets for the military, announced Friday it has transferred the launch to a Falcon 9 rocket from SpaceX, ULA’s chief rival in the market for launching US government satellites.

This is only the latest example of the Space Force moving a GPS launch from ULA to SpaceX. The three most recent GPS satellites were also supposed to launch on ULA’s Vulcan rocket. Beginning in 2024, the Space Force shifted them over to SpaceX. In exchange, military officials moved three future launches from SpaceX to ULA, including the launch of the GPS III SV10 satellite.

ULA’s Vulcan rocket is now grounded for the second time in less than two years, prompting the Space Force to move GPS III SV10 back to SpaceX. ULA will receive rights to launch a classified US military mission in 2028.

Space Systems Command assigned launch missions for the final four GPS satellites several years ago. At the time, the Vulcan rocket was nearing its first test flight. The debut was delayed from 2020 until early 2024, and since then, the new rocket’s path to operational service has not been smooth. On its second and fourth flights—in October 2024 and last month—the new rocket suffered problems with its solid rocket boosters.

An investigation into the first incident found a manufacturing defect was responsible for the loss of the booster’s exhaust nozzle shortly after liftoff. Officials have not announced the results of the probe into the second booster failure, but it appeared to be similar to the first incident. On each flight, the Vulcan rocket recovered from the anomaly and successfully completed its climb into orbit.

The recurrence of the same type of failure twice in four flights gave US military officials pause. Last month, the military officer in charge of the Space Force’s space launch program said the investigation into the booster problem will take “many months.” The Space Force won’t resume launching payloads on the Vulcan rocket until the investigation is complete.

Those statements suggested the Space Force was likely to transfer the GPS slated to fly on the next Vulcan rocket to a different launch vehicle. That’s exactly what happened. On Friday, Space Systems Command confirmed that GPS III SV10 will now launch on SpaceX’s Falcon 9 rocket in late April.
